title: Theory of distribution skewness effect on polydisperse random close packing
authors: Vinay Vaibhav, Carmine Anzivino, Alessio Zaccone
abstract: We investigate the random close packing density, $φ_\textrm{RCP}$, of polydisperse hard sphere systems using a theoretical framework based on the equilibrium model of crowding. We derive a closed-form solution for $φ_\textrm{RCP}$ in terms of the moments of the diameter distribution, enabling an analytical exploration of the effects of polydispersity ($δ$) and skewness ($S$) on packing density. For a binary mixture, it is possible to explore a broader range of dependence of $φ_\textrm{RCP}$ on $δ$ for a given $S$ or on $S$ for a given $δ$. We show that the dependencies of $φ_\textrm{RCP}$ on skewness for a variety of continuous distributions collapse onto a theoretical master curve obtained for the binary mixture case. By correcting the theory so that it obeys known exact limiting behaviours for extreme size asymmetry, our analytical predictions not only agree with previously obtained numerical results, but also predict previously unexplored regions of the $φ_\textrm{RCP}$ parameter space.
summary: This paper provides a theoretical framework to calculate the random close packing density of polydisperse hard spheres by incorporating the effects of size distribution skewness and polydispersity. Using an extension of the Percus-Yevick equation, the authors derive closed-form predictions that show the results collapse onto master curves, providing a quantitative link between particle size statistics and jamming density.
main_problem: The paper investigates the random close packing density ($\phi_{RCP}$) of polydisperse hard sphere systems, focusing on how particle size distribution moments, particularly skewness ($S$), influence this density.
main_result: The authors derive a closed-form theoretical prediction for $\phi_{RCP}(\delta, S)$ that shows all size distributions sharing the same moments collapse onto a master curve, agreeing with existing numerical results.
method: The study employs a theoretical framework based on the equilibrium model of crowding, extending the Percus–Yevick virial equation of state for mixtures using a three-moment approximation.
model_or_system: The system modeled is polydisperse hard spheres, which can be extended to bidisperse mixtures. The theory relates the packing density to the moments of the particle diameter distribution.
key_observables: Random close packing density ($\phi_{RCP}$), dependence on polydispersity ($\delta$) and skewness ($S$).
important_parameters: Polydispersity ($\delta$), Skewness ($S$), and the moments of the diameter distribution ($\langle\sigma^nangle$).
main_assumptions: The primary assumptions include using a truncatable three-moment description of the mixture equation of state and approximating local crowding by the equilibrium state.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates $\phi_{RCP}$ dependence on $\delta$ (fixed $S$) and $S$ (fixed $\delta$) for various distributions, showing collapse onto the analytical prediction. Figure 2 compares analytical predictions across different distributions against literature numerical estimates.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theoretical framework by extending the PY equation of state, derives the closed-form solution for $\phi_{RCP}(\delta, S)$, corrects the theory for asymptotic limits, and then validates the predictions by comparing them against numerical results for various size distributions.
why_interesting: While focused on classical packing, the rigorous statistical mechanical treatment of density functionals and the use of moment expansions for complex systems are relevant to understanding phase behavior in many-body quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Variation of random close packing fraction with polydispersity and skewness. ϕRCP as a function of (a) polydispersity δ for different values of skewness S, as indicated, and (b) as a function of skewness S for different values of polydispersity δ, as indicated. Solid lines correspond to the analytical prediction ϕRCP(δ, S) obtained from the three–moment theory (see text and Supplementary Information [32]), while symbols denote the same function evaluated for different continuous size distributions: beta (open circles), generalized gamma (open squares), log-normal (closed triangles), and truncated power-law (closed diamonds).
